EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough pulling anomalously high moisture content into the region resulted a line of high based shower activity across south-central AZ. Strong evaporational cooling from these showers resulted in strong winds and consequently blowing dust issues, with the most significant issue near Casa Grande, AZ where visibilities near zero were observed along I-10. A vehicular crash from the dust storm was observed along the interstate, resulting in an injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility in blowing dust on I-10 in Casa Grande. There was also a traffic accident during the dust storm with one person transported to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A retrograding strong ridge of high pressure resulted in temperatures climbing well above normal across the Phoenix area, while a trough was situated to the west along the West Coast. The hottest days in Phoenix during this time occurred on the 20th and 21st as highs topped out at 115 degrees and 117 degrees, respectively. The 117 degree high on the 21st tied the record high for the date. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 18 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two direct fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as location, age, gender, and circumstances unavailable.

A quick storm system impacted northern Arizona tonight through Thursday with strong winds and widespread snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow was reported 8 miles west of Chino Valley at an elevation of 4,800 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick storm system impacted northern Arizona tonight through Thursday with strong winds and widespread snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow was 8 inches at Sawmill Chapter house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active monsoon day produced severe thunderstorms in the Vail area on the southeast side of Tucson which resulted in severe threshold winds and flash flooding. Another thunderstorm also produced severe threshold winds and property damage in Sierra Vista.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station recorded a wind gust of 64 mph. Broadcast media reported 2 trees down that also damaged the roofs of 2 houses near Highway 90 and Schraeder Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of severe thunderstorms including a confirmed tornado occurred in Cochise County. Severe wind gusts, hail and flash flooding also occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station recorded a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rolled through portions of the Tucson Metro resulting in strong winds and localized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable water running along low water crossing on N. Thornydale Rd. just south of W. Hardy Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter weather system hit northern Arizona beginning early January 7 through early January 8.  This cold system lowered snow levels as low as 3000 feet elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagstaff airport received 8 inches of snow by noon on January 7.  The total for the day was 10.8 inches. Other snow amounts (in inches): Parks 11.7,  Kachina Village 9.5 to 12.8, Munds Park 10.5, Williams 9.0, NWS Bellemont office 8.8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving winter weather system brought widespread snow to the higher elevations of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of new snow fell in the community of Wheatfields. Seventeen inches of new snow fell 12 NNW Sawmill and 10 inches fell in Sawmill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving winter weather system brought widespread snow to the higher elevations of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sunrise Park Ski Resort measured 10.0 inches of snow in 24 hours at an elevation of 10,000 feet. Eight inches of snow fell 5 miles east of Pinetop-Lakeside (at about 7,200 feet elevation).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen inches of snow fell Forest Lakes. Eight inches of new snow fell in Heber-Overgaard. Nine inches of new snow fell 13 miles east-northeast of Clints Well in the Blue Ridge area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving over northern Arizona brought high winds to east central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust at the Taylor Airport was 59 MPH at 235 PM.  The sustained wind was above 40 MPH between 155 PM and 235 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving over northern Arizona brought high winds to east central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust at a wind sensor 5 miles N-NW of Sunrise Park (MP 378 on SR-260) was 61 MPH at 1634 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front tied to the upper level low pressure system north of Arizona brought active weather to northern Arizona on March 13. Convective showers and thunderstorms brought rain, snow, and graupel. Along the high country corridors of I-40 and I-17, snow squalls (often mixed with graupel) caused vehicle accidents and traffic slowdowns. ||The upper level low moved into the Lower Colorado River Valley on March 14 and lingered in that general vicinity through March 15 with bands of low elevation rain and high elevation snow. The morning of March 15 was rather active.  The low weakened after that with less and less impact over north central Arizona. Snow continued in northeastern Arizona until early on March 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 9 inches of snow fell overnight Saturday to early Sunday morning in Tsaile. Estimated 12 inches of snow overnight Saturday to early Sunday morning in Wheatfields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity developed across the higher terrain and foothills north and east of the Phoenix metro area during the afternoon hours of the 1st and moved southwestward towards the Phoenix East Valley before weakening. The thunderstorms were instigated by large scale forcing for ascent from an upper-level low moving over southern AZ as well as MLCAPE values of 500 J/KG. DCAPE values were in excess of 1500 J/KG and thus conditions were ideal for these thunderstorms to produce strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was measured at Falcon Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cutoff upper-level low was situated over the Lower Colorado River Valley area on the 18th, bringing some unsettled weather to parts of south-central Arizona. SBCAPE in excess of 500 J/kg along with steep mid-level lapse rates led to thunderstorms producing hail around 0.75. No damage was reported with these thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area during the late morning through the afternoon hours. Strong winds with occasional gusts in excess of 40 mph were reported with the frontal passage. The strong winds caused a large palm tree to fall on a man that was walking in Highline Park in Phoenix, killing him.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large palm tree fell directly on a man walking in Highline Park in Phoenix, killing him. There was a peak wind gust of 45 mph reported by a Citizen Weather Observer Site located nearby.

Additionally, heat was a contributing factor of death for an individual in Tucson on July 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving across the Desert Southwest combined with well-above normal moisture, with precipitable water values peaking at 1.0-1.2, resulted in a large swath of rain with embedded thunderstorms moving across portions of southeast CA and southwest AZ during the afternoon hours on the 22nd. Rainfall rates with this band ranged between 0.10-0.50 according to doppler radar estimates, resulting in total amounts of between 1-1.25 within a period of three hours. The rainfall was enough to result in areas of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avenue A by 28th Street was closed due to a storm drain shooting water up and causing the road to flood. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving across the Desert Southwest combined with well-above normal moisture, with precipitable water values peaking to around 0.8, resulted in multiple rounds of light to moderate rainfall with embedded thunderstorms across south-central AZ from the 21st to 23rd. Rainfall rates during these multiple rounds of rain generally ranged between 0.10-0.50, resulting in storm total amounts of between 0.5-3 across the region during a 72-hour period. The highest rainfall amounts were observed across portions of western Pinal and southwest Maricopa Counties, where there were reports of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Agate Road wad closed from South Olivine Road to South Amarillo Valley Road due to running washes with the road washed out at the intersection with South Amarillo Valley Road. No injuries were reported.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought category continued across far southern and eastern sections of southeast Arizona during much of January. The area near the New Mexico line encompassed southern Greenlee, southeast Graham and eastern Cochise counties, including the Chiricahua Mountains; while areas near the International Border stretched from the Huachuca Mountains westward across Santa Cruz County to the Buenos Aires National Wildlife Refuge in southeast Pima County. By the end of the month, drought conditions improved to severe (D2) for most of these locales except near the New Mexico border in southern Greenlee, southeast Graham and eastern Cochise counties. ||Rain occurred on numerous occasions in January, with above normal rain in Pima and Santa Cruz Counties. January monthly totals ranged from 0.5 to 3.0, with the highest amounts in the mountains and from Tucson westward. In the areas of extreme drought, along the New Mexico border, rain amounts were generally 25 to 75 percent of normal. Elsewhere, where conditions improved by the end of the month, rain amounts were 75 to 150 percent of normal. Soil moisture readings was in the 20th to 50th percentile ranking in most areas through January.

The area near the New Mexico line encompassed southern Greenlee, southeast Graham and eastern Cochise counties, including the Chiricahua Mountains; while areas near the International Border stretched from the Huachuca Mountains westward across Santa Cruz County to the Buenos Aires National Wildlife Refuge in southeast Pima County. By the end of the month, drought conditions improved to severe (D2) for most of these locales except near the New Mexico border in southern Greenlee, southeast Graham and eastern Cochise counties. ||Rain occurred on numerous occasions in January, with above normal rain in Pima and Santa Cruz Counties. January monthly totals ranged from 0.5 to 3.0, with the highest amounts in the mountains and from Tucson westward. In the areas of extreme drought, along the New Mexico border, rain amounts were generally 25 to 75 percent of normal. Elsewhere, where conditions improved by the end of the month, rain amounts were 75 to 150 percent of normal. Soil moisture readings was in the 20th to 50th percentile ranking in most areas through January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level low moved onto the West Coast around the Monterey Bay area. This brought southerly winds gusting from 40 to 50 mph across much northern Arizona. Heavy precipitation was observed in the frontal band as it moved across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the Western Mogollon Rim. The top reports were 8.9 to 12 inches in the Parks area and 8.5 near Hart Prairie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving across the Desert Southwest resulted in shower and thunderstorm activity across most of south-central AZ during the afternoon hours on the 7th. A landspout was generated from a thunderstorm cell moving just south of Casa Grande, AZ. The landspout developed over an open field area near the Casa Grande Mountains with no damages nor injuries reported. Landspout lasted for about 8 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visual photos showed a weak landspout developed over an open field near the Casa Grande Mountains just south of Casa Grande, AZ from a thunderstorm cell moving over the area. The landspout lasted for about 8 minutes with no evidence of any damages or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system diving down from the north brought snow and isolated impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two roads were closed 5 miles NNW of Hualapai Peak due to snow. Based on nearby obs, approximately two inches of snow had fallen at the time the roads were closed, and it was still snowing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold low pressure system diving down from the north brought isolated impacts from snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two roads were closed 5 miles NNW of Hualapai Peak at 0726 MST due to snow. Based on estimates from cameras in the area, approximately two inches of snow had fallen at the time of the closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms moved over northern Arizona with nearly continuous snow in some of the higher elevations for several days. The snow level started around 7000 feet then dropped to as low as 3,500 by late in the event. Higher elevations received two to three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started falling over the Grand Canyon Country during the morning of February 6. By 0800 on the 7th, eight inches had fallen at the North Rim. Over 21 inches (21.5) of new snow fell at the North Rim by 1100 AM on the 9th. The South Rim had 10.7 by the time the snow stopped.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm with heavy mountain snow and strong winds impacted the Sky Islands of southeast Arizona late February 1 through February 2, 2024. This produced about a foot of snow above 7000 feet along with wind gusts in excess of 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 12 inches of snow occurred in Hannagan Meadow and surrounding areas of northern Greenlee County. Wind gusts also exceeded 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm with heavy mountain snow and strong winds impacted the Sky Islands of southeast Arizona late February 1 through February 2, 2024. This produced about a foot of snow above 7000 feet along with wind gusts in excess of 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 12 inches of snow occurred in Mount Graham. Wind gusts also exceeded 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm with heavy mountain snow and strong winds impacted the Sky Islands of southeast Arizona late February 1 through February 2, 2024. This produced about a foot of snow above 7000 feet along with wind gusts in excess of 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 12 inches of snow occurred in the Summerhaven area and surrounding Catalina Mountains. Wind gusts also exceeded 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low brought rain and strong winds to the region on the 6th. Strong gusts in excess of 40 mph were observed across parts of the Phoenix metro with Phoenix Sky Harbor recording a peak gust of 46 mph. These strong winds led to a large Palo Verde tree toppling over onto a parked vehicle, resulting in minor damage. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds resulted in a large Palo Verde tree toppling over onto a parked car sitting in a parking lot near Frye Road and Chandler Village Drive. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system centered along the Arizona/Mexico international border combined with adequate moisture and instability (MUCAPE around 500 J/kg) led to thunderstorms across parts of southeast Arizona. Thunderstorms in Pinal County generated a gusty outflow boundary that traveled northward toward the Phoenix area. The outflow generated dense blowing dust along parts of I-10 going into the Phoenix metro with visibilities dropping below a quarter mile in a few areas across south Phoenix. No accidents were reported as a result of the dense blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense blowing dust led to 1/4 mile visibility near I-10 and Riggs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system moved through the region on February 6 and 7 resulting in strong gusty winds and heavy mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 59 MPH occurred at the Tucson International Airport. Additional, minor wind damage reports inclusive of minor roof damage occurred in Vail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system moved through the region on February 6 and 7 resulting in strong gusty winds and heavy mountain snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three feet of new snow fell in Forest Lakes in less than three days. Eleven inches of snow fell in Heber.  Just over nine inches (9.2) fell 4 miles south-southwest of Shumway by 400 PM on February 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms moved over northern Arizona with nearly continuous snow in some of the higher elevations for several days. The snow level started around 7000 feet then dropped to as low as 3,500 by late in the event. Higher elevations received two to three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started to fall around 800 AM on February 6 across the White Mountains. McNary picked up 8 inches of snow by 800 AM on the 7th and 14 inches by 800 PM.  The Pinetop-Lakeside area received 10 to 15 inches of new snow by 800 PM on the 7th. Sunrise Ski Park received 29 inches of new snow over 3 days. Nutrioso had a three day total of 7.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms moved over northern Arizona with nearly continuous snow in some of the higher elevations for several days. The snow level started around 7000 feet then dropped to as low as 3,500 by late in the event. Higher elevations received two to three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the greater Prescott area over a three day period. Snow started falling during the evening of February 6.  By 300 AM, some locations above 5000 feet received 8 inches of snow. Snow totals for the three day event ranged from 5 inches north of (lower than Prescott) to over 20 inches northwest of (higher than) Prescott. Groom Creek received 10.3 inches of new snow by 900 AM on the 7th. Crown King received 9 inches by 8 PM on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms moved over northern Arizona with nearly continuous snow in some of the higher elevations for several days. The snow level started around 7000 feet then dropped to as low as 3,500 by late in the event. Higher elevations received two to three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started falling across the Western Mogollon Rim around 630 AM on February 6.  By 730 PM, Parks had received 8.5 inches of snow. The snow continued to fall into the evening of February 9th. Here are some of the three day snowfall totals in inches: Parks 36.0, Flagstaff 32 to 36.1, Williams 30.0,  areas surrounding Flagstaff 11.6 to 28.9,  and Munds Park 28.6.  A location 3 miles south of Mormon Lake received 14 inches of snow by 900 AM on February 7.  Schools were closed in the area.  Many tree branches and trees fell in the Flagstaff under the weight of the snow. There were spotty power outages as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms moved over northern Arizona with nearly continuous snow in some of the higher elevations for several days. The snow level started around 7000 feet then dropped to as low as 3,500 by late in the event. Higher elevations received two to three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started falling in Washington Park (5,400 feet) at 1100 PM on February 6. Twenty-nine inches fell by the time the snow stopped late morning on February 9th. Strawberry received 24 inches and Pine received 14 inches of snow over 3 days. A location 4 miles south of Washington Park received 15.5 inches of snow in 2 days (by the afternoon of the 8th).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms moved over northern Arizona with nearly continuous snow in some of the higher elevations for several days. The snow level started around 7000 feet then dropped to as low as 3,500 by late in the event. Higher elevations received two to three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started falling over the Kaibab Plateau during the morning of February 6. By 0800 on the 7th, eight inches had fallen at the North Rim. Over 21 inches (21.5) of new snow fell at the North Rim by 1100 AM on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms moved over northern Arizona with nearly continuous snow in some of the higher elevations for several days. The snow level started around 7000 feet then dropped to as low as 3,500 by late in the event. Higher elevations received two to three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell 3 miles east of Gray Mountain by 830 AM on February 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms moved over northern Arizona with nearly continuous snow in some of the higher elevations for several days. The snow level started around 7000 feet then dropped to as low as 3,500 by late in the event. Higher elevations received two to three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several snow squalls produced 4.2 inches of snow at about 4,500 feet elevation in Sedona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moving across the Desert Southwest transported well-above normal moisture across the region with PWATs peaking at between 0.8-1 across southwestern AZ. The combination of the forcing for ascent from the trough and the abundant moisture resulted in waves of showers throughout the day, with some areas in southwest AZ observing rainfall amounts upwards of an inch over a several hour period based on doppler radar estimates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Salome Road reported closed due to water over roadway. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two atmospheric river storm systems brought a heavy plume of oceanic moisture to Arizona which caused heavy snow to fall over the higher terrain for parts of three days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the Western Mogollon Rim from West. The Flagstaff Airport and WFO Flagstaff received close to ten inches of new snow.  Kachina Village received 8 inches and Munds Park received 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated shower and thunderstorm activity formed near the Catalina Mountains with some light shower activity in NW Tucson. One of these showers resulted in a dry microburst that caused at least 5 power poles to be downed at Ina Rd and Shannon Rd. These power poles also knocked over the traffic lights at this intersection. Cars were damaged and trapped in the heavily traveled Ina Rd and Shannon Rd intersection area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry microburst resulted in 14 downed power poles were destroyed, multiple falling into on coming traffic, of which two power poles landed on cars injuring and trapping people inside, and significantly damaging the cars. Significant power outages resulted in over 1000 costumers without power for an extended amount of time. The Utility company (TEP) reported taking 4 to 5 days to rebuild their infrastructure as a result of the impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low moved across The Great Basin and through Arizona. This system was anomalously strong for the time of year and is quite cold aloft.  The  pressure gradient tightened up and resulted in extremely gusty and strong winds.  While most areas experienced wind gusts around 40-50 MPH with some areas had gusts  of 55-65 MPH.  The highest gusts were downwind of the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind sensor at Highway 260 (mile post 378) and Highway 273 reported a peak wind gust of 62 MPH at 413 PM. The sustained wind was above 40 MPH and/or wind gusts were above 58 MPH between1130 PM and 430 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low moved across The Great Basin and through Arizona. This system was anomalously strong for the time of year and is quite cold aloft.  The  pressure gradient tightened up and resulted in extremely gusty and strong winds.  While most areas experienced wind gusts around 40-50 MPH with some areas had gusts  of 55-65 MPH.  The highest gusts were downwind of the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mogollon Rim Airpark measured a peak wind gust of 59 MPH at 335 PM. The Mesonet station near Heber measure a peak wind gust of 63 MPH. The RAWS station at Limestone Canyon had a peak wind gust of 63 MPH at 124 PM. Wind gusts at Limestone were 58 MPH or higher from around 100 PM to 300 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual moisture over northern Arizona was enough for isolated showers and thunderstorms to develop this afternoon over parts of northern Arizona. One storm produced a brief landspout tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report with photo from social media of a brief landspout tornado. Time and location             estimated from radar.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system brought high winds across parts of northern for two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor along I-40 at The Little Colorado River measured a peak wind gust of 59 MPH at 413 PM. There was also a 58 MPH wind gust at 523 PM. The peak wind gust at the Winslow Airport was 71 MPH at 516 PM. The wind gusts were 58 MPH or higher between 320 PM and 540 PM. Sustained winds were above 40 MPH between 300 PM and 740 PM.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system brought high winds across parts of northern for two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Springerville Airport had a peak wind gust of 66 MPH at 735 PM.  The station reported sustained winds of at least 40 MPH and/or wind gusts higher than 58 MPH between 610 and 740 PM. There was a second bought of high wind gusts above 58 MPH between 1130 PM and 1230 AM with a second peak gust of 63 MPH at 1135 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front tied to the upper level low pressure system north of Arizona brought active weather to northern Arizona on March 13. Convective showers and thunderstorms brought rain, snow, and graupel. Along the high country corridors of I-40 and I-17, snow squalls (often mixed with graupel) caused vehicle accidents and traffic slowdowns. ||The upper level low moved into the Lower Colorado River Valley on March 14 and lingered in that general vicinity through March 15 with bands of low elevation rain and high elevation snow. The morning of March 15 was rather active.  The low weakened after that with less and less impact over north central Arizona. Snow continued in northeastern Arizona until early on March 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 9 inches of snow fell overnight Saturday to early Sunday morning in Tsaile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of very intense thunderstorms moved from southeast to northwest through the Tucson Metro area. The storm was most intense from midtown Tucson extending to the northwest into portions of Marana. Wind damage was extensive across the much of the Tucson Metro with an estimate of thousands of fallen trees, numerous power poles down, building damage to several buildings, widespread sign damage, roof damage and vehicle damage due to fallen trees. This event also resulted in 1 to 2.5 inches of rain and numerous swift water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three quarter inch hail was reported in the Vail and Corona De Tucson area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front tied to the upper level low pressure system north of Arizona brought active weather to northern Arizona on March 13. Convective showers and thunderstorms brought rain, snow, and graupel. Along the high country corridors of I-40 and I-17, snow squalls (often mixed with graupel) caused vehicle accidents and traffic slowdowns. ||The upper level low moved into the Lower Colorado River Valley on March 14 and lingered in that general vicinity through March 15 with bands of low elevation rain and high elevation snow. The morning of March 15 was rather active.  The low weakened after that with less and less impact over north central Arizona. Snow continued in northeastern Arizona until early on March 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started falling in Vernon around noon on March 13.  Eight inches fell by 0800 on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm complex, likely instigated from upper-level ascent from an upper-level low moving northward along the Baja Peninsula, developed over Pima County during the early afternoon hours. A strong outflow boundary emanating from the thunderstorm complex then migrated westward across southwestern Maricopa and Yuma Counties in southwest AZ, with areas of dense blowing dust observed. A Yuma Proving Ground Station reported visibilities down to less than one tenth of a mile at one point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of 0.08 of a mile measured at the Yuma Proving Ground Station 5, located northeast of Roll, AZ, due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human caused fire called the Watch Fire began on July 10th near San Carlos. On the 11th, thunderstorms developed over the White Mountains and along the Mogollon Rim before descending toward the southwest across southern Gila County. MLCAPE upwards of around 1,000 J/kg along with DCAPE values upwards of 1,500-2,000 J/kg promoted an environment conducive to producing strong downdrafts and thus leading to strong outflows. During the afternoon on the 11th, strong thunderstorms moving toward the southwest across southern Gila County produced strong outflow winds in excess of 30-40 mph. The strong winds led to the rapid spread of the Watch Fire into southeast portions of San Carlos, causing multiple structures to be destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Watch Fire began on the 10th before rapidly spreading during the afternoon of the 11th due to strong thunderstorm outflow winds in excess of 30-40 mph. The strong winds caused the fire to rapidly spread into southeast portions of San Carlos, destroying at least 21 homes and 13 auxiliary structures. The fire became 100 percent contained on the 17th after burning more than 2,000 acres. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under northeasterly flow due to high pressure situated near southern Nevada, thunderstorm activity, fueled by 500-1000 J/KG of MLCAPE, developed along the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains and moved southwestward towards Gila County and most of southeastern AZ. Activity weakened before reaching the south-central AZ lower deserts due to strong convective inhibition. DCAPE values, however, were near 2000 J/KG across the lower deserts of south-central AZ and thus conditions were favorable for long traveling deep outflow boundaries. One such outflow boundary emanating from higher terrain activity affected the Phoenix area, with the strongest winds observed across the Phoenix East Valley where gusts upwards of 50 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Awning blown apart from one house with the garage roof ripped off from another home from a mobile home park unit located near the area of East Broadway Road and 86th street in East Mesa. Peak wind gusts were estimated at around 50 mph with timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first triple digits of the season occurred starting May 27 in Tucson through the end of the month. The warmest day occurred on May 28 with a high of 103 in Tucson. Hot temperatures occurred throughout southeast Arizona. On May 28, Moderate Heat Risk category was reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the cause of death in one individual.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures around 6 degrees above normal were experienced in the Tohono O'odham Nation May 28. Highs of around 100-103 degrees occurred with moderate Heat Risk levels.  The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary factor in the death of one individual on the Tohono O'odham Nation along a walking path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Needles Fire started in the 900 block of Bush Street and moved north, jumping the Colorado River into Mohave Valley. The fire eventually burned 203 acres - 60 in CA and 143 in AZ - and destroyed one structure in CA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire began in Needles, CA at 1215 PST/1315 MST, burning one structure, and jumped the Colorado River into Mohave Valley at 1340 PST/1440 MST. The fire was stopped at a total of 203 acres (60 acres in CA and 143 in AZ) at 1530 PST/1630 MST. There was no structural damage reported on the AZ side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of very intense thunderstorms moved from southeast to northwest through the Tucson Metro area. Wind damage was extensive across the much of the Tucson Metro with an estimate of thousands of fallen trees, numerous power poles down, building damage to several buildings, widespread sign damage, roof damage and vehicle damage due to fallen trees. This event also resulted in 1 to 2.5 inches of rain and numerous swift water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain ranging from about 1 to 2.5 inches resulted in about 5 swift water rescues across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures typically 7 to 11 degrees above normal were experienced across southeast Arizona July 8-12. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two individuals found in the desert, eight in the Tucson area, two in the Safford area and another in Rio Rico. Heat was determined to be a contributing factor in the death of twelve individuals in the Tucson area and another near Sells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 8 to 11 degrees above normal were experienced in the Tucson Metro Area July 8 through July 12, causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached. Tucson International Airport topped out each day between 109 and 112 degrees. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary factor in the death of eight individuals and a contributing factor in the deaths of twelve individuals.

Multiple trees down around the metro area as well from the saturated soils. Tucson Fire Department responded to over 25 calls with 15 of those calls relating to stranded drivers or stuck in swift moving water. Two adults were found dead in a wash after a swift water rescue attempt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many reports of downed trees across the city of Tucson. Estimated wind gusts of 45 mph. Likely scenario is that winds were strong enough combined with saturated ground due to very heavy rain to result in many trees being downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first active convective day of the 2024 monsoon produced several thunderstorms that resulted in strong winds and flash flooding. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust to 61 mph at Pioneer Airfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low pressure system moved across the western United States, dragging a cold front across the region. Strong westerly winds behind the cold front resulted in areas of dense blowing dust, mainly across portions of Pinal County near Arizona City where visibilities of less than a quarter mile were observed. The dense blowing dust resulted in multiple car collisions, forcing the closure of Sunland Gin Road.  Peak wind gusts in excess of 30 mph were measured based on nearby surface observations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple vehicle collisions resulted in the closure of Sunland Gin Road from Battaglia Drive to Mountain View Avenue near Arizona City to due to dense blowing dust, with visibilities less than a quarter mile observed. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity developed across much of eastern AZ, along the higher terrain features, during the early afternoon hours. Outflow boundaries emanating from the activity over the higher terrain areas sparked a complex of robust thunderstorms over Pinal County. A northwestward moving outflow boundary that developed from the Pinal County storms produced wind gusts in excess of 50 mph as well as areas of dense blowing dust across the Phoenix East Valley. Additional thunderstorm activity then developed over central Phoenix during the late afternoon, likely instigated by a convergence zone that set up right over the area with little convective inhibition in place and thus it did not take much of a triggering mechanism to get convective initiation. With MLCAPE values of 1000-1500 J/KG along with DCAPE of around 1500 J/KG, conditions were favorable for the generation of strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm downburst winds broke a window at the Maricopa County Department of Emergency Management facility as well as shattered a window of a vehicle. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 60-70 mph. No injuries were reported.

Heat was determined to be a contributing factor in the death of twelve individuals in the Tucson area and another near Sells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 7 to 10 degrees above normal were experienced on the Tohono O'odham Nation July 8 through July 11 causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary factor in the death of one individual on July 8 and another on July 11. Additionally, heat was a contributing factor in the death of one individual on July 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance moving across southern AZ aided in providing the necessary forcing for ascent to result in scattered to locally numerous thunderstorm activity to develop across southwest AZ during the afternoon hours. The combination of MLCAPE values of 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG resulted in a favorable environment for the generation of strong to severe downburst winds from these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of multiple power poles down, causing a power outage, near Salome. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a moisture surge from a large thunderstorm complex over northern Sonora, Mexico and an upper-level trough moving across the Pacific Northwest resulted in the development of thunderstorm activity across south-central AZ during the early morning hours on the 18th. With MLCAPE values near 500 J/KG and DCAPE values of around 1000 J/KG, conditions were favorable for the generation of strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple downed trees around the Fountain Park area in Fountain Hills. A 61 mph gust was measured. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona with dew points ranging from |50-65F, with precipitable water ranging from 1.50 to 0.75 - 0.92.  Atmospheric instability was on the higher side as well. The highest CAPE values are expected along and south of the Mogollon Rim where values are forecast to range from 1000 J/kg to 1700 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cienega Creek rose at least three feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture over northern Arizona and slow steering winds allowed thunderstorms to produce localized heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged roof shingles and window screens near Cornville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture over northern Arizona and slow steering winds allowed thunderstorms to produce localized heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of several roads in the Cornville area, including Cornville Road near Cactus Road was seen on a social media video. Another video showed property and street flooding east of Page Springs Road and north of Cornville Road. There was also flooding along Harmony, Oak Run Lane, and Page Springs Road. Flooding of multiple homes along Alvey and Brooks Lanes. The fire department personnel reported at least 6 homes flooded in Cornville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a moisture surge from a large thunderstorm complex over northern Sonora, Mexico and an upper-level trough moving across the Pacific Northwest resulted in the development of thunderstorm activity across south-central AZ during the early morning hours on the 18th. With MLCAPE values near 500 J/KG and DCAPE values of around 1000 J/KG, conditions were favorable for the generation of strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees, estimated between 100-150, reported across the TPC Scottsdale Champions Golf Course. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity developed across much of eastern AZ, along the higher terrain features, during the early afternoon hours. Outflow boundaries emanating from the activity over the higher terrain areas sparked a complex of robust thunderstorms over Pinal County. A northwestward moving outflow boundary that developed from the Pinal County storms produced wind gusts in excess of 50 mph as well as areas of dense blowing dust across the Phoenix East Valley. Additional thunderstorm activity then developed over central Phoenix during the late afternoon, likely instigated by a convergence zone that set up right over the area with little convective inhibition in place and thus it did not take much of a triggering mechanism to get convective initiation. With MLCAPE values of 1000-1500 J/KG along with DCAPE of around 1500 J/KG, conditions were favorable for the generation of strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail reported along Highway 60 at Top of the World. No damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm activity, fueled by MLCAPE values of around 1000 J/KG, developed across the Mogollon Rim and the White Mountains and were steered westward into Gila County. The strong thunderstorm activity produced rainfall amounts in excess of 1.5 inches in less than one hour, enough to result in flash flooding along the Sevenmile Wash, located northwest of San Carlos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sevenmile Wash rose approximately 2 ft, making low water crossings across the wash impassable. Timing estimated from radar estimated precipitation. No damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture over northern Arizona and Utah brought showers and thunderstorms to those areas. This caused several spikes to the Paria River at the confluence of the Colorado River.  There also were several spikes in the flow in the days before this episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over far northern Arizona and south-central Utah caused several spikes on the Paria River just before it joined the Colorado River upstream from The Grand Canyon. The water flow started to increase (from 4.98 feet) around 100 PM MST on August 13.  There were three crests in the flow: 6.24 feet at 500 PM, 704 feet at 800 PM MST on August 13, and 7.66 feet at 300 AM MST on August 14. The flow returned to near normal at around 1100 PM on August 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture over easern Arizona allowed at least one thunderstorm with heavy rain to form over the Little Colorado River basin above St Johns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused an incrase in the water flowing down the Little Colorado River near St Johns.  The flow started to incrase at 1500 and peaked at around 2 feet higher at 1700. The river dropped back to near normal at 2100.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of an area of high pressure was located near the Four Corners, a near |perfect location for the monsoon moisture to continue spreading over northern Arizona. This brought the continuation of daily afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms to the area. The PWATs ranged from around 0.90-1.40, which is slightly above the climatological normals for early August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A USGS river gauge on Oak Creek in Sedana measured a two foot rise in 15 minutes from heavy rain near Munds Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon moisture continued with daily showers and thunderstorms over northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail (1.00 inch diameter) was reported 6 miles southeast of Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of severe thunderstorms including a confirmed tornado occurred in Cochise County. Severe wind gusts, hail and flash flooding also occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser live streamed video of tornado in the distance. Location estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure system centered over the Four Corners Region, the flow across the region was from the southeast. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, particularly over Pima County in southeast AZ, and were steered northwestward towards western Maricopa County with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values of 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, resulted in an environment conducive to strong to severe downburst winds. Some of the thunderstorm activity was also slow moving, resulting isolated flash flooding, with the area of Cruff Wash south of Buckeye reaching bankfull and resulting in flooding along low-water crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station located about 30 miles south of Gila Bend measured a 70 mph wind gust from thunderstorm downburst winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity developed across much of eastern AZ, along the higher terrain features, during the early afternoon hours. Outflow boundaries emanating from the activity over the higher terrain areas sparked a complex of robust thunderstorms over Pinal County. A northwestward moving outflow boundary that developed from the Pinal County storms produced wind gusts in excess of 50 mph as well as areas of dense blowing dust across the Phoenix East Valley. Additional thunderstorm activity then developed over central Phoenix during the late afternoon, likely instigated by a convergence zone that set up right over the area with little convective inhibition in place and thus it did not take much of a triggering mechanism to get convective initiation. With MLCAPE values of 1000-1500 J/KG along with DCAPE of around 1500 J/KG, conditions were favorable for the generation of strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Webcam showed reduced visibility below one quarter mile along the Superstition Freeway and Gilbert Road due to dense blowing from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms traversed portions of northern Arizona early in the morning with rainfall rates around 0.10-0.30 inches per hour.  There was a brief lull in activity around daybreak to mid morning. The the second round of widespread showers and thunderstorms developed by the late morning into early afternoon.  Dynamics from an approaching trough helped to enhance storm activity during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over the Catract Creek and Havasu Creek drainages.  Radar estimated  1-2 of rain fell early in the afternoon of August 22nd. Flash flooding occurred which resulted in many people being evacuated from the campgrounds on the creek, with helicopter evacuations for people stranded at the remote village of Supai.  At least 6 homes had flood damage in the Village of Supai. Over 100 campers were stranded in the campground. One person was washed away near the confluence with the Colorado River. Her body was recovered over 18 miles down the Colorado River three days later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees in the area of Shea Boulevard and Loop 101 in Scottsdale. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity developed across much of eastern AZ, along the higher terrain features, during the early afternoon hours. Outflow boundaries emanating from the activity over the higher terrain areas sparked a complex of robust thunderstorms over Pinal County. A northwestward moving outflow boundary that developed from the Pinal County storms produced wind gusts in excess of 50 mph as well as areas of dense blowing dust across the Phoenix East Valley. Additional thunderstorm activity then developed over central Phoenix during the late afternoon, likely instigated by a convergence zone that set up right over the area with little convective inhibition in place and thus it did not take much of a triggering mechanism to get convective initiation. With MLCAPE values of 1000-1500 J/KG along with DCAPE of around 1500 J/KG, conditions were favorable for the generation of strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An adult male was found deceased due to drowning at Canyon Lake. The man was paddle boarding at the time the thunderstorm was approaching and fell off the paddleboard likely due to the strong winds and higher waves. A surface observing site located at Canyon Lake measured a peak gust of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure system centered over the Four Corners Region, the flow across the region was from the southeast. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, particularly over Pima County in southeast AZ, and were steered northwestward towards western Maricopa County with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values of 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, resulted in an environment conducive to strong to severe downburst winds. Some of the thunderstorm activity was also slow moving, resulting isolated flash flooding, with the area of Cruff Wash south of Buckeye reaching bankfull and resulting in flooding along low-water crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the Maricopa County Flood Control District Gauge located at Cruff Wash, water levels rose above the alarm level stage of 2.5 feet at around 1952MST and crested at 3.91 feet, which is above the bank full level stage of 3.88 feet, at around 2015MST. Water levels fell below alarm stage at around 2105MST. Based on the information of problem areas produced by the Maricopa County Flood Control District, flooding was likely along the low water crossing of Agua Caliente Road located above 2 miles downstream of the gauge. No damages nor injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Maricopa County Flood Control District Gauge located along Indian Bend Wash at McDonald Drive in Scottsdale measured water levels rising above the alarm stage level of 2.08 feet at around 7:50 PM MST on the 22nd and staying above that level through the next several hours before falling below alarm stage at around 6:50 AM MST on the 23rd. Therefore, several low-level water crossings intersecting the wash downstream  likely had flooding with the most significant impacts occurring along Roosevelt Street, where there was a video of a vehicle driving into the wash and stalling. No significant damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture, an easterly wave, and unseasonable jet stream dynamics fueled scattered thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. One storm produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 389 was flooded with water, mud, and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure system situated across northern AZ, the steering flow was from the east. Riding underneath the subtropical high was an easterly disturbance near the International Border, which helped enhance the easterly flow at mid-levels upwards of 25 kts as well as the forcing for ascent. MLCAPE values between 1000-1500 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG resulted in an environment favorable for strong to severe downburst winds. The enhanced easterly flow, which increased the vertical shear upwards of 25-30 kts, contributed to the overall storm organization and thus for most of the thunderstorm activity to become severe. Thunderstorm activity first developed across the higher terrain areas of eastern and southeastern AZ with additional activity developing over the lower deserts via outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines down in the Tonto Basin area, affecting approximately 1338 customers. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

The bulk of the wind damage occurred along the area of SR-95 to Riggles Ave and Senter Street to Sunset Street, including damaged or destroyed sheds and sheet metal car ports, roof damage, flipped and destroyed RVs, several downed or snapped trees, and at least one downed power pole. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 60-70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture over northern Ariozna, very weak steering flow, and westerly winds provided ventilation aloft leading to slow-moving thunderstorms with locally heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The rive guage on Granite Creek in Prescott measured over a 6.5 foot rise in one hour betweeb 400 PM and 500 PM. The creek slowly dropped to near normal levels by 500 AM early the next morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved across Southeastern Arizona causing uprooted trees in SE Tucson area and in the Benson area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Uprooted tree at the Loves Truck Stop at the intersection of Interstate 10 and Highway 90 found on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved across Southeastern Arizona causing uprooted trees in SE Tucson area and in the Benson area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees uprooted in neighborhood. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moderately unstable trough moved over northern Arizona with areas of rain and snow. Most areas saw light to moderate accumulations. A few areas received heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just under 8 inches of snow (7.7 inches) fell at 8,160 feet elevation in the past 24 hours 4  miles NW Arizona Snowbowl.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moderately unstable trough moved over northern Arizona with areas of rain and snow. Most areas saw light to moderate accumulations. A few areas received heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in and around Vernon. A foot of snow was reported in Vernon and 6-8 inches of snow was reported 9 miles west of Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dust devil hit the Williams Fairground, caused damage, and injured three people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As people were trying to set up a hot air balloon, a dust devil created winds estimated to be between 40-50 mph. This caused the balloon to detach from its anchor point, sending it into nearby buildings. Three bystanders were injured, including an 8-year-old girl with a broken thumb and rope burns, a 15-year-old who sustained neck and back injuries, and an adult woman who was cut. The balloon also caused damage to two parked cars, knocked down a light pole, and damaged electrical equipment in a nearby building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture began to increse over Yavapai County which lead to the development of a severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail was reported 4 miles southwest of the Prescott Airport and 1/2 to 7/8 inch diameter hail was reported in Prescott.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall went through the Tucson Metro on August 8th resulting in 0.5 to 1.0 inches of rain leading to flash flooding and running washes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall went through the Tucson Metro on August 8th resulting in 0.5 to 1.0 inches of rain leading to flash flooding and running washes. In addition, the Pima County Medical Examiner found that drowning was the primary factor in the death of one individual. The individual was found in Aviation Wash on August 9th with debris and running water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms across Cochise County and Santa Cruz County resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of flash flooding on roadways and stranded vehicles near Washington Ave and Golf Course Road in Douglas. There was also a car stuck in flooded roadway at Primrose St and Washington Ave in Douglas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of severe thunderstorms moved through the Tucson Metro. This produced severe threshold winds and damage in both the southeast and west to northwest sides of the Tucson Metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station recorded a wind gust of 62 mph. Additionally, these thunderstorm winds produced tree damage and damage to mobile homes near I-10 and Kolb Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low diving southeastward through southern Nevada and across northern Arizona with instability and upward motion brought widespread showers, thunderstorms, snow, and strong winds. The frontal band moved across northeastern Arizona during the afternoon with CAPE values near 500 J/kg and sufficient deep layer wind shear to produce strong to locally severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold air funnel was observed near Tuba City at 500 PM. Unable to verify if it touched down at any point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low diving southeastward through southern Nevada and across northern Arizona with instability and upward motion brought widespread showers, thunderstorms, snow, and strong winds. The frontal band moved across northeastern Arizona during the afternoon with CAPE values near 500 J/kg and sufficient deep layer wind shear to produce strong to locally severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado or landspout was observed southwest of Tuba City near Kerley Valley. Verified by video.

The frontal band moved across northeastern Arizona during the afternoon with CAPE values near 500 J/kg and sufficient deep layer wind shear to produce strong to locally severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust of 61.7 MPH at Mesonet Station AZT18 (9124 feet) was measured at 1033 AM.  Other wind gusts of near or over 58 MPH were occasionally reported until around 245 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high wind to the lee side of the White Mountains in the eastern Little Colorado River Valley in Springerville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS station KJTC at Springerville Airport reported a peak wind gust of 66 MPH at 815 AM. The sustained wind was above 40 MPH frequently between 755 AM and 135 PM. The wind gusted to 58 MPH or higher at times between 755 AM and 115 PM MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS station QLCA3 at Limestone Canyon reported a peak wind gust of 67 MPH at 1024 AM. Wind gusts of 60 MPH or higher were reported until 1224 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low diving southeastward through southern Nevada and across northern Arizona with instability and upward motion brought widespread showers, thunderstorms, snow, and strong winds. The frontal band moved across northeastern Arizona during the afternoon with CAPE values near 500 J/kg and sufficient deep layer wind shear to produce strong to locally severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: BB to pea sized hail covered the ground and Interstate 40 an inch or two deep. At least |seven vehicles slid off the road. Several vehicles crashed into each other.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across eastern and southeastern AZ during the afternoon hours. Deep and colliding outflows sparked new development across the lower deserts of south-central AZ during the early evening hours, including several thunderstorm clusters developing over the Phoenix metro area and producing strong to severe downburst winds. The environment that these thunderstorms developed under was characterized by MLCAPE values of over 1000 J/KG with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, which were favorable parameters for severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large warehouse facility located in the area of West Van Buren Street and North 47th Avenue in Phoenix collapsed with multiple walls and portion of roof missing. A person was trapped underneath the rubble and found deceased approximately 2.5 days later by a search and rescue operations team. Peak wind gust estimated by doppler radar was upwards of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough moved from the Great Basin and through the Desert Southwest. This feature combined with anomalously high moisture content for late April resulted an area of showers moving from southern Nevada through the Lower Colorado River Valley while weakening during the early to mid-afternoon hours. A strong outflow resulting from these weakening showers caused strong winds, destroying some sheds in the Parker Strip area. The SPC mesoanalysis showed DCAPE values of around 1000 J/Kg, which was favorable for the strong wind generation from the outflow boundary that moved through. Based on the magnitude of the damage observed, wind gusts were estimated to be upwards of 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong outflow from collapsing showers moving through the area resulted in strong winds across Parker Strip. The strong winds destroyed some sheds, with the metal roofing becoming entangled around a power pole, knocking power out. Based on the magnitude of the damage observed, wind gusts were estimated to be upwards of 50 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system moving across the Desert Southwest resulted in large scale forcing for ascent with highly anomalous moisture in place. This result in an area of moderate to locally heavy rain to develop over the Phoenix metro area with many areas receiving between 0.5-1. The rainfall was enough to cause Indian Bend Wash, located in Scottsdale, to reach action stage and thus cause some flooding along some low water crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Maricopa County Flood Control District Gauge at Indian Bend Wash near Shea Boulevard reported water levels rising above alarm stage, which is 2 feet, at 3:30 am MST, peaking at around 2.31 feet (1055 cfs) at 6:00 am MST. Water levels dropped below alarm stage by 8:30 am MST. The intersection of North Scottsdale Road and Indian Bend Road was closed due to flooding from the elevated water levels from the wash. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm activity developed across the higher terrain areas and foothills to the north and east of Phoenix during the afternoon and early evening hours. A storm complex that developed across the higher terrain areas to the northeast of Phoenix sent a surging outflow boundary westward into the Phoenix metro area with gusty winds in excess of 40 mph as well as areas of blowing dust observed. The gusty winds were enough to cause some minor tree and property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inch tree branch from a large tree broke off near the intersection of Price and Baseline Roads in Tempe. Wind gusts were estimated at between 40-50 mph based on nearby observing sites. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong thunderstorms developed over western Pinal County, particularly in the area between Casa Grande and Arizona City, during the late afternoon into the early evening hours. The overall environment the thunderstorms developed under was characterized by weak to moderate instability with CAPE values ranging between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, which was favorable for strong to severe downburst winds to materialize.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bottom three rows of the house roof tile were blown off along with the cement brick privacy walls between houses knocked over near the area of North Pinal Avenue and East McCartney Road in Casa Grande. Wind gusts were estimated at between 55 and 60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong thunderstorms developed over western Pinal County, particularly in the area between Casa Grande and Arizona City, during the late afternoon into the early evening hours. The overall environment the thunderstorms developed under was characterized by weak to moderate instability with CAPE values ranging between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, which was favorable for strong to severe downburst winds to materialize.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof of a 30 foot by 40 foot steel shed blown off near the area of West Val Vista Boulevard and I-10 in Casa Grande. Wind gusts were estimated at around 55 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southeasterly flow, resulted in a surge of moisture across the region with PWATs rising to between 1.0-1.2. The moisture in combination with strong daytime heating lead to CAPE values to rise to between 1000-1500 J/KG. As a result, thunderstorms developed across the higher terrain areas to the east of Phoenix from northeast Pinal through extreme eastern Maricopa and southern Gila Counties. With DCAPE values of around 2000 J/KG, the environment was very favorable for the generation of strong downburst winds reaching severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 64 mph wind gust was measured at the San Carlos RAWS station. Timing estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm complex that developed over Yavapai County during the afternoon hours sent a southward surging outflow boundary across western portions of the Phoenix metro area. Wind gusts from the outflow boundary based on surrounding observing sites were estimated in the 40-50 mph range, which was enough to cause some minor tree damage, specifically in the Glendale area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palo verde tree uprooted due to strong winds from outflow boundary at the Fry's Supermarket parking lot located near the intersection of 43rd Avenue and Bell Road in Glendale. Wind gusts were estimated at 40-50 mph based on nearby surface observations.

The overall environment the thunderstorms developed under was characterized by weak to moderate instability with CAPE values ranging between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, which was favorable for strong to severe downburst winds to materialize.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New construction of an RV Garage located in Calle Vistoso in Casa Grande was destroyed with the damage blown nearly 1/4 mile away. Wind gusts were estimated at between 55 and 60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system just west of the area brought in highly anomalous moisture into the region, with PWAT values surging to as much as 0.8-1. The moisture combined with the dynamical forcing from the low pressure resulted in widespread high-based showers/virga to develop across much of south-central AZ during the afternoon hours. Given the very dry sub-cloud layer, DCAPE values ranged between 1000-1500 J/KG across much of the region and thus the environment was favorable for the generation of strong winds from these showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Buckeye Municipal Airport measured a 61 mph gust from a virga shower that moved overhead. Nearby, in the community of Tartesso, a small shed was thrown far from a home onto a street. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure centered near the Lower Colorado River Valley, the steering flow pattern across much of eastern and central AZ was from the north to northeast. Thunderstorm activity developed across the Mogollon Rim during the late morning into the early afternoon hours and was steered south to southwestward into south-central AZ. An upper-level disturbance moving in from New Mexico helped enhance the vertical ascent and thus resulted in the thunderstorm coverage to become quite numerous with deep and colliding outflow boundaries helping to spark new activity across the lower deserts of south-central AZ. MLCAPE values were in the 1000-1500 J/KG range, with DCAPE values of around 1500 J/KG. Thus the parameters were favorable for these thunderstorms to generate strong to severe downburst winds. The steering flow was very light, resulting in slow storm motions and thus heavy rainfall leading to isolated instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power poles in the area of Polk Street and 295th Avenue in Buckeye. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 70 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure situated over the Four Corners, the steering flow across AZ was from the southeast. This southeasterly flow advected greater moisture content into the area with PWAT values surging to 1.5+ inches. The moisture combined with the daytime heating resulted in MLCAPE values in excess of 1000 J/KG. A strong to severe thunderstorm complex developed over southeastern portions of AZ by the late afternoon hours and then migrated northwestward toward south-central AZ during the evening hours with outflow winds generating areas of dense blowing dust as well as triggering additional thunderstorm activity over portions of the Phoenix metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree brought down a carport in the area of Frank Lloyd Wright Boulevard and North Scottsdale Road in Scottsdale. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure situated over southern Nevada, the steering flow was from the northeast. Thunderstorm activity developed over the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours and was steered southwestward towards Yavapai, northwestern Maricopa, and La Paz Counties. The overall environment was characterized by MLCAPE values of 500-1000 J/KG, with DCAPE values near 2000 J/KG. Thus, conditions were favorable for strong to severe downburst wind gusts to materialize with any thunderstorm activity that developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in Aguila measured a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure centered near Las Vegas, Nevada, the overall steering flow was from the north to northeast. Strong daytime heating in combination with PWAT values of 1.5+ inches resulted in MLCAPE values in excess of 1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG. In addition, in the upper-levels, there was a disturbance that helped with the vertical ascent. As a result, the environment was primed for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms. Strong thunderstorm activity developed across the Mogollon Rim during the late morning into the early afternoon hours and was steered southwestward towards south-central AZ. A deep, long traveling outflow boundary developed and surged well ahead of the activity and traversed the entire Phoenix metro area with strong winds as well as areas of blowing dust during the mid to late afternoon hours. The outflow boundary as it continued to migrate southward sparked additional thunderstorm activity across portions of northwest Pinal and southwest Maricopa Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station located approximately 11 miles west of Gila Bend measured a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure centered near Las Vegas, Nevada, the overall steering flow was from the north to northeast. Strong daytime heating in combination with PWAT values of 1.5+ inches resulted in MLCAPE values in excess of 1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG. In addition, in the upper-levels, there was a disturbance that helped with the vertical ascent. As a result, the environment was primed for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms. Strong thunderstorm activity developed across the Mogollon Rim during the late morning into the early afternoon hours and was steered southwestward towards south-central AZ. A deep, long traveling outflow boundary developed and surged well ahead of the activity and traversed the entire Phoenix metro area with strong winds as well as areas of blowing dust during the mid to late afternoon hours. The outflow boundary as it continued to migrate southward sparked additional thunderstorm activity across portions of northwest Pinal and southwest Maricopa Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility down to 1/4 mile reported in the area of SR-347 near the Gila River crossing. No accidents nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong thunderstorms developed over western Pinal County, particularly in the area between Casa Grande and Arizona City, during the late afternoon into the early evening hours. The overall environment the thunderstorms developed under was characterized by weak to moderate instability with CAPE values ranging between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, which was favorable for strong to severe downburst winds to materialize.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four power poles down on Overfield Road near the intersection with Battaglia Drive in Arizona City. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong thunderstorms developed over western Pinal County, particularly in the area between Casa Grande and Arizona City, during the late afternoon into the early evening hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Concrete shingle tiles torn from the side of a roof as well as several concrete fence structures blown over in Las Montanas Subdivision in Casa Grande. Wind gusts were estimated at between 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure centered near the Lower Colorado River Valley, the steering flow pattern across much of eastern and central AZ was from the north to northeast. Thunderstorm activity developed across the Mogollon Rim during the late morning into the early afternoon hours and was steered south to southwestward into south-central AZ. An upper-level disturbance moving in from New Mexico helped enhance the vertical ascent and thus resulted in the thunderstorm coverage to become quite numerous with deep and colliding outflow boundaries helping to spark new activity across the lower deserts of south-central AZ. MLCAPE values were in the 1000-1500 J/KG range, with DCAPE values of around 1500 J/KG. Thus the parameters were favorable for these thunderstorms to generate strong to severe downburst winds. The steering flow was very light, resulting in slow storm motions and thus heavy rainfall leading to isolated instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles down near Buckeye Rd and 339th Ave west of Buckeye. Winds gusts were estimated upwards of 70 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across eastern and southeastern AZ during the afternoon hours. Deep and colliding outflows sparked new development across the lower deserts of south-central AZ during the early evening hours, including several thunderstorm clusters developing over the Phoenix metro area and producing strong to severe downburst winds. The environment that these thunderstorms developed under was characterized by MLCAPE values of over 1000 J/KG with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, which were favorable parameters for severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple commercial buildings located in the area of Roosevelt Street and North 44th Avenue in Phoenix had most of their solar panels ripped off their roofs and pieces of the buildings were scattered around. Trees 60-80 feet tall were knocked down. Semi-trucks were flipped on their sides. Peak wind gust based on doppler radar estimates was estimated upwards of 75 mph. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail reported in the area 19th Avenue and Southern Avenue in Phoenix. No damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure situated over the Four Corners, the steering flow across AZ was from the southeast. This southeasterly flow advected greater moisture content into the area with PWAT values surging to 1.5+ inches. The moisture combined with the daytime heating resulted in MLCAPE values in excess of 1000 J/KG. A strong to severe thunderstorm complex developed over southeastern portions of AZ by the late afternoon hours and then migrated northwestward toward south-central AZ during the evening hours with outflow winds generating areas of dense blowing dust as well as triggering additional thunderstorm activity over portions of the Phoenix metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three downed power poles were reported in the area of South Stanfield Road and West Peters Road in Stanfield. Wind gusts were estimated at between 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The typical afternoon thunderstorm activity developed across the higher terrain areas of northern and eastern AZ. Most the activity remain anchored over the high terrain with barely any activity observed across the lower elevations given the strong convective inhibition in place. However, with MLCAPE values of around 1000 J/KG, the environment was still favorable for any thunderstorm that developed to become strong. A strong, nearly stationary thunderstorm developed during the evening hours over the Wildcat Burn Scar in northern Maricopa County, dumping rainfall amounts in excess of 1.5 in less than a hour. This was enough rainfall in a short time period to result in flood issues in the burn scar area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over Bartlett Dam Road at Camp Creek. Heavy rain fell on the Wildcat Fire Burn Scar with 1.65 inches of rain recorded in less than one hour at the Camp Creek at Bartlett Dam Road rain gage operated by Maricopa County Flood Control. The County Flood Control notified Maricopa County Department of Transportation to barricade road. No damages nor injuries were reported.

High temperatures in Phoenix during this time peaked at 113 degrees on both June 6th and 7th, setting a new record high on the 6th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 15 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong instability plume set up across the southeastern half of AZ with MLCAPE values upwards of 1000 J/KG. Simultaneously, an upper-level trough moving across the Pacific Northwest resulted in a corridor of strong vertical ascent. The combination of the available instability and upper-level forcing resulted in widespread thunderstorm development across southeast AZ with some of the activity moving into portions of southern Gila County, where heavy rainfall was observed across Gilson Wash, west of the San Carlos area. Doppler radar estimated rainfall amounts were in excess of an inch in less than hour, enough to result in water rises along Gilson Wash and flooding along the low-water crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stream gauge along Gilson Wash recorded an approximate 2.38 ft jump, impacting downstream low water crossings. No damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low near the northern Baja Peninsula provided strong forcing for ascent across much of the southern half of AZ. This upper-level forcing in combination with MLCAPE values approaching 1000 J/KG, resulted in the development of strong thunderstorm activity across much of southern AZ with additional activity developing in the Yuma area. DCAPE values were in excess of 1500 J/KG and thus conditions were favorable for these thunderstorms to produce strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power poles and power lines across I-8 between Fortuna Rd and Foothills Blvd. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.
