EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least three semi-trucks flipped over near Warner Enterprises located in the area of West Lower Buckeye Road and South 49th Avenue in Phoenix. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 75 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 12th, a new wildfire named the Rose Fire developed on a riverbed approximately 5 miles southeast of Wickenburg, AZ. The fire was able to spread somewhat and cause the closure of U.S. Highway 60 with several structures and vehicles nearby destroyed. Fire was able to be contained approximately 72 hours after its initial start with a total of nearly 200 acres burned. Very dry and hot conditions, with temperatures in the triple digits, along with breezy conditions with gusts of around 15 mph were observed during the time the fire ignited. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire named the Rose Fire developed about 5 miles southeast of Wickenburg, AZ along a river bed near U.S. Highway 60. The fire jumped on the other side of the highway, forcing its closure. The fire caused evacuations of people living along the homes situated nearby. At the end, the blaze destroyed 15 structures, seven which were primary residences, along with 12 vehicles, a horse trailer, and a recreational vehicle. In total, the fire consumed nearly 200 acres. No injuries were reported.

Deep and colliding outflows sparked new development across the lower deserts of south-central AZ during the early evening hours, including several thunderstorm clusters developing over the Phoenix metro area and producing strong to severe downburst winds. The environment that these thunderstorms developed under was characterized by MLCAPE values of over 1000 J/KG with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, which were favorable parameters for severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site located at the Phoenix International Raceway in Avondale measured a 66 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across eastern and southeastern AZ during the afternoon hours. Deep and colliding outflows sparked new development across the lower deserts of south-central AZ during the early evening hours, including several thunderstorm clusters developing over the Phoenix metro area and producing strong to severe downburst winds. The environment that these thunderstorms developed under was characterized by MLCAPE values of over 1000 J/KG with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, which were favorable parameters for severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm tree snapped in half in the area of Buckeye Road and North 54th Avenue in Phoenix. Winds gusts were estimated upwards of 75 mph based on doppler radar estimates. No  injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure centered near Las Vegas, Nevada, the overall steering flow was from the north to northeast. Strong daytime heating in combination with PWAT values of 1.5+ inches resulted in MLCAPE values in excess of 1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG. In addition, in the upper-levels, there was a disturbance that helped with the vertical ascent. As a result, the environment was primed for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms. Strong thunderstorm activity developed across the Mogollon Rim during the late morning into the early afternoon hours and was steered southwestward towards south-central AZ. A deep, long traveling outflow boundary developed and surged well ahead of the activity and traversed the entire Phoenix metro area with strong winds as well as areas of blowing dust during the mid to late afternoon hours. The outflow boundary as it continued to migrate southward sparked additional thunderstorm activity across portions of northwest Pinal and southwest Maricopa Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Arizona Department of Transportation webcam along Loop 303 at Olive Avenue just northwest of Luke Air force Base showed visibility down to 1/4 mile due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure situated over the Four Corners, the steering flow across AZ was from the southeast. This southeasterly flow advected greater moisture content into the area with PWAT values surging to 1.5+ inches. The moisture combined with the daytime heating resulted in MLCAPE values in excess of 1000 J/KG. A strong to severe thunderstorm complex developed over southeastern portions of AZ by the late afternoon hours and then migrated northwestward toward south-central AZ during the evening hours with outflow winds generating areas of dense blowing dust as well as triggering additional thunderstorm activity over portions of the Phoenix metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility down to a quarter of a mile reported in the area of 32nd street and University Drive in Phoenix due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents nor injuries were reported.

The environment that these thunderstorms developed under was characterized by MLCAPE values of over 1000 J/KG with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, which were favorable parameters for severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Traffic light support pole fully bent in the opposite direction in the area of Roosevelt Street and North 51st Avenue in Phoenix. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 75 mph based on doppler radar estimates. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure centered near the Lower Colorado River Valley, the steering flow pattern across much of eastern and central AZ was from the north to northeast. Thunderstorm activity developed across the Mogollon Rim during the late morning into the early afternoon hours and was steered south to southwestward into south-central AZ. An upper-level disturbance moving in from New Mexico helped enhance the vertical ascent and thus resulted in the thunderstorm coverage to become quite numerous with deep and colliding outflow boundaries helping to spark new activity across the lower deserts of south-central AZ. MLCAPE values were in the 1000-1500 J/KG range, with DCAPE values of around 1500 J/KG. Thus the parameters were favorable for these thunderstorms to generate strong to severe downburst winds. The steering flow was very light, resulting in slow storm motions and thus heavy rainfall leading to isolated instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station located at the Phoenix Raceway in Avondale measured a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall pattern was characterized by a subtropical high pressure system situated over the southern Plains while an upper-level trough was moving across the Intermountain west, resulting in a southwesterly steering flow. Moisture was plentiful with PWAT values in excess of 1.5 across most of south-central AZ. This moisture combined with daytime heating resulted in MLCAPE values peaking at around 1000 J/KG. As a result, the instability combined with orographic lifting resulted in the development of widespread thunderstorm activity across the higher terrain areas just to the east of Phoenix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large eucalyptus tree branches were snapped off at the Boyce Thompson Arboretum in Superior. Wind gusts were estimated at between 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure situated over the Four Corners, the steering flow across AZ was from the southeast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-Seven downed power poles along Pinnacle Peak Road in North Phoenix. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 65 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low near the northern Baja Peninsula provided strong forcing for ascent across much of the southern half of AZ. This upper-level forcing in combination with MLCAPE values approaching 1000 J/KG, resulted in the development of strong thunderstorm activity across much of southern AZ with additional activity developing in the Yuma area. DCAPE values were in excess of 1500 J/KG and thus conditions were favorable for these thunderstorms to produce strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three power poles snapped along Ave 5E between County 13th St and 14 St. Winds were estimated upwards of 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over the Desert Southwest allowed for temperatures to climb well above normal on the 22nd and 23rd. The hottest temperatures occurred on the 22nd when Phoenix officially recorded a high of 102 degrees, which was 15 degrees above the daily normal high and 3 degrees short of the record high. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, three heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One direct fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm activity developed across the higher terrain areas and foothills to the north and east of Phoenix during the afternoon and early evening hours. A storm complex that developed across the higher terrain areas to the northeast of Phoenix sent a surging outflow boundary westward into the Phoenix metro area with gusty winds in excess of 40 mph as well as areas of blowing dust observed. The gusty winds were enough to cause some minor tree and property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter mile visibility observed along Loop 202 between Loop 101 and 44th street in the Tempe and Scottsdale area due to blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over the Desert Southwest resulted in excessive heat conditions during the middle part of June. Temperatures in Phoenix topped out above 110 degrees on the 11th and 12th before lowering several degrees on the 13th and 14th as a low pressure system moved through the Southwest. Following the passage of the weather system, temperatures once again spiked above 110 degrees on the 15th and 16th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 25 heat-associated fatalities were reported during this time. Additionally, three heat-associated fatalities were reported in La Paz County as well as one heat-associated fatality in southern Gila County.

A strong ridge of high pressure amplified over the western CONUS during the early part of June, resulting in well above normal temperatures and excessive heat conditions. High temperatures in Phoenix during this time peaked at 113 degrees on both June 6th and 7th, setting a new record high on the 6th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 15 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five direct fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

A retrograding strong ridge of high pressure resulted in temperatures climbing well above normal across the Phoenix area, while a trough was situated to the west along the West Coast. The hottest days in Phoenix during this time occurred on the 20th and 21st as highs topped out at 115 degrees and 117 degrees, respectively. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over the Desert Southwest resulted in excessive heat conditions during the middle part of June. Temperatures in Phoenix topped out above 110 degrees on the 11th and 12th before lowering several degrees on the 13th and 14th as a low pressure system moved through the Southwest. Following the passage of the weather system, temperatures once again spiked above 110 degrees on the 15th and 16th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 25 heat-associated fatalities were reported during this time. Additionally, three heat-associated fatalities were reported in La Paz County as well as one heat-associated fatality in southern Gila County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One indirect fatality was reported in La Paz County, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm activity developed across the higher terrain areas and foothills to the north and east of Phoenix during the afternoon and early evening hours. A storm complex that developed across the higher terrain areas to the northeast of Phoenix sent a surging outflow boundary westward into the Phoenix metro area with gusty winds in excess of 40 mph as well as areas of blowing dust observed. The gusty winds were enough to cause some minor tree and property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tents became airborne and large limbs fell from a tree in the center median of 107th Ave intersecting with Happy Valley Road in Surprise. Wind gusts were estimated at between 40-50 mph based on nearby observing sites. No injuries were reported.
